did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0), to perform multi-class skin disease classification on the HAM10000 dermoscopy dataset. The EfficientNet-B0 model achieved 92.4% accuracy and an AUC-ROC of 0.961.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning framework for multi-class skin disease detection. It details specific methodologies including transfer learning with ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0 on the HAM10000 dataset, reports new quantitative results (e.g., 92.4% accuracy), and describes the deployment of a Flask web application. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Disease Detection Using Deep Learning
journal: International Journal for Research in Applied Science & Engineering Technology (IJRASET)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of automated multi-class skin disease detection by developing a deep learning framework using transfer learning with ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0 architectures. The models were fine-tuned on the HAM10000 dermoscopy dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven lesion categories, while addressing severe class imbalance through data augmentation and class-weighted loss functions. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing the performance of these three architectures using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. The EfficientNet-B0 model achieved the highest overall classification accuracy of 92.4% with an AUC-ROC of 0.961. To demonstrate practical utility, the system was deployed via a Flask web application for real-time prediction. The authors conclude that this approach represents a meaningful step toward accessible, AI-assisted dermatology, particularly in resource-constrained settings.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for multi-class classification. It is the majority class with 6,705 samples (approx. 67% of dataset). EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.964 and AUC-ROC=0.980.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset. It is a malignant class with 1,113 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.905 and AUC-ROC=0.963. Noted as clinically critical.

One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset with 1,099 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.898 and AUC-ROC=0.958. Frequently confused with Melanoma (22 misclassifications as Melanoma, 18 as Benign Keratosis).
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset with 514 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.870 and AUC-ROC=0.951.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset with 327 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.848 and AUC-ROC=0.944.

One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset, a minority class with 142 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.862 and AUC-ROC=0.956. Performance described as 'particularly clean' with few off-diagonal entries.
One of the seven classes in the HAM10000 dataset, a minority class with 115 samples. EfficientNet-B0 achieved F1=0.834 and AUC-ROC=0.941.

all_task_types.target: Seven clinically relevant skin disease categories (Melanocytic Nevi, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesions, and Dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification using transfer learning with ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0 on the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs a 7-dimensional probability vector via a Softmax layer, and the predicted class is determined by argmax. The primary evaluation metric is overall classification accuracy (92.4% for EfficientNet-B0) along with per-class precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy dataset compiled by Tschandl et al. and made available through the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermoscopic images collected from different acquisition sites over 20 years, labelled by consensus expert dermatologist review. The dataset includes metadata such as patient age, gender, and lesion localisation, though this study focused exclusively on image-based classification.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, EfficientNet-B0) after applying a 70:15:15 stratified split. Data augmentation (flips, rotations, zoom, brightness/contrast jitter, shearing) was applied to minority classes during training.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (15% of the dataset) to monitor convergence and trigger early stopping.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of model performance (15% of the dataset) to calculate accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC, and compared qualitatively across architectures without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNet-B0 achieved the highest overall accuracy (92.4%) with significantly fewer parameters (5.3M) compared to ResNet-50 (25.6M) and VGG-16 (138M). The most notable misclassifications occurred between Melanoma and Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions (22 Melanoma cases misclassified as Benign Keratosis; 18 Benign Keratosis cases as Melanoma). Data augmentation improved F1-scores for minority classes (Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesions, Dermatofibroma) by 0.15 to 0.30 points. Early stopping triggered at epoch 47, with best checkpoint at epoch 43.

architecture_details.backbone_structure: 7 Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks with depthwise separable convolutions, squeeze-and-excitation attention, and skip connections
architecture_details.normalization: Batch Normalization (in backbone), Batch Normalization (after Dense Layer 1)

training_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Two-phase: 1) Freeze all pre-trained layers, train custom head. 2) Unfreeze last 30% of convolutional layers, train with reduced learning rate
training_details.data_augmentation: Random horizontal/vertical flips, rotations up to 30 degrees, zoom 0.8-1.2, brightness/contrast jitter, horizontal shearing

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels to conform with transfer learning architecture input dimensions
Pixel value normalization to the range [0, 1] by dividing by 255
Per-channel mean subtraction using ImageNet mean values to accelerate convergence
Stratified sampling for dataset partitioning into training, validation, and test sets (70:15:15 split)

Data augmentation for minority classes during training: random horizontal and vertical flips, rotations up to 30 degrees, random zoom (0.8 to 1.2), brightness and contrast jitter, and horizontal shearing

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied exclusively to minority classes during training as part of augmentation-based oversampling.
Applied up to 30 degrees to minority classes during training.
Applied in the range of 0.8 to 1.2 to minority classes during training.
Applied to minority classes during training.
Applied to minority classes during training.
Used for class balancing augmentation by assigning higher penalty to misclassifications of minority classes based on inverse class frequency weights.

conclusions: The fine-tuned EfficientNet-B0 model achieved the highest overall classification accuracy of 92.4% and an AUC-ROC of 0.961 on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming ResNet-50 (91.1% accuracy) and VGG-16 (88.7% accuracy).

EfficientNet-B0 demonstrated superior computational efficiency, requiring significantly fewer parameters (5.3 million) compared to ResNet-50 (25.6 million) and VGG-16 (138 million), making it suitable for resource-constrained environments.

Data augmentation and class-weighted loss functions effectively addressed severe class imbalance, improving F1-scores for minority classes (Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesions, and Dermatofibroma) by 0.15 to 0.30 points without degrading performance on majority classes.
The model performed strongest on Melanocytic Nevi (F1 = 0.964) and Melanoma (F1 = 0.905), which are the most clinically critical categories.

The most notable source of misclassification occurred between Melanoma and Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions, with 22 Melanoma cases misclassified as Benign Keratosis and 18 Benign Keratosis cases as Melanoma, reflecting known overlapping dermoscopic features.

The system was successfully deployed via a lightweight Flask web application, demonstrating practical utility for real-time prediction and accessibility in settings where specialist dermatological care is difficult to access.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is a known benchmark with severe class imbalance (Melanocytic Nevi ~67%) and potential selection bias from specific acquisition sites over 20 years; external validation on independent, diverse populations is absent.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare model performances; differences between EfficientNet-B0 (92.4% accuracy) and ResNet-50 (91.1% accuracy) are reported descriptively without significance testing.

The paper does not report per-class sensitivity/specificity trade-offs or calibration metrics beyond AUC-ROC, which limits assessment of clinical utility for rare but critical classes like Melanoma (F1=0.905) and Dermatofibroma (F1=0.834).

Deployment is limited to a lightweight Flask web application; there is no discussion of integration with Electronic Health Records (EHR), regulatory compliance (e.g., FDA, CE marking), or real-world clinical workflow integration.

The model was trained and evaluated on dermoscopic images only; it does not account for variations in image quality, lighting, or non-dermoscopic clinical photographs which are common in real-world settings.

While data augmentation improved F1-scores for minority classes by 0.15-0.30, the paper acknowledges that Melanoma and Benign Keratosis-Like Lesions remain frequently confused (22 and 18 misclassifications respectively), highlighting persistent diagnostic ambiguity in the data.

The authors are from a single institution (Meerut Institute of Engineering and Technology), and the work is published in IJRASET, which may raise concerns regarding independent peer review rigor compared to top-tier medical AI journals.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images) dermoscopy dataset, compiled by Tschandl et al. and available via the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). The dataset contains 10,015 images across seven lesion categories. The dataset was split using stratified sampling into a training set (70%), a validation set (15%), and a test set (15%). The models were pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned on the HAM10000 training set. The validation set was used for monitoring convergence and early stopping. The test set was used for final performance evaluation. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset. The authors explicitly note in the limitations that external validation on independent, diverse populations is absent.

training.notes: Used for fine-tuning ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0. Data augmentation (flips, rotations, zoom, jitter, shearing) and class-weighted loss were applied to address class imbalance.

validation.notes: Used for monitoring convergence during training and triggering early stopping (triggered at epoch 47, best checkpoint at epoch 43).

testing.notes: Used for final evaluation of model performance. Maintained original class distribution.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: Used for pre-training the convolutional bases of ResNet-50, VGG-16, and EfficientNet-B0 before fine-tuning on HAM10000. Per-channel mean subtraction using ImageNet mean values was applied during preprocessing.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation techniques (random horizontal/vertical flips, rotations up to 30 degrees, random zoom 0.8-1.2, brightness/contrast jitter, horizontal shearing) were applied exclusively to minority classes during training to balance the dataset.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 70:15:15 stratified split of the HAM10000 dataset into training, validation, and test sets respectively.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The test set was a held-out portion of the same HAM10000 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: EfficientNet-B0 outperformed ResNet-50 (91.1% accuracy) and VGG-16 (88.7% accuracy). Per-class F1-scores ranged from 0.834 (Dermatofibroma) to 0.964 (Melanocytic Nevi).
Lower performance compared to EfficientNet-B0 and ResNet-50.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which has known severe class imbalance and potential selection bias from specific acquisition sites over 20 years.
External validation on independent, diverse populations is absent, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other clinical settings or demographics.
The model was trained and evaluated on dermoscopic images only; it does not account for variations in image quality, lighting, or non-dermoscopic clinical photographs common in real-world settings.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to compare model performances.
information_not_reported: Patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient appear in both train and test sets).
Specific counts for each class in the training, validation, and test splits (only total counts and overall class distribution are provided).
Confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
